Sangoli Rayanna

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Sangoli Rayanna

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 15, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

Share

In the quiet folds of Northern Karnataka lies **Kittur**, a town that once roared with rebellion. Long before 1857 etched itself into textbooks, **Rani Chennamma of Kittur** stood tall in 1824 — a lone queen challenging the might of the East India Company.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BY2n!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F27d7733f-9a20-4189-8388-f114cba74530_281x180.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BY2n!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F27d7733f-9a20-4189-8388-f114cba74530_281x180.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WgJD!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F73fcf5a3-efad-4b60-802c-f18f9da319f2_480x480.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WgJD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F73fcf5a3-efad-4b60-802c-f18f9da319f2_480x480.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Born in the village of **Kakati**, nestled in the heart of Karnataka, **Chennamma** belonged to a proud **Lingayat** family — rooted in spiritual strength and social reform. At just **14**, she was married to **Raja Mallasarja**, ruler of Kittur, stepping into royal life with grace and quiet resolve.

In **1824**, the same year her husband passed away, her **young son too left the world**, leaving Chennamma not just widowed, but childless — a queen without an heir, a mother without a child.

When the British sought to exploit her grief through the *Doctrine of Lapse*, Chennamma adopted **Shivalingappa** and declared him heir. Her refusal to surrender was not just political — it was personal, sacred, and fierce. But **St John Thackeray**, the British collector of Dharwad, dismissed it as *fraud*, wielding colonial law like a blade against native legitimacy.

Chennamma wrote to **Mountstuart Elphinstone**, the Lieutenant Governor of Bombay Presidency — not with submission, but with reason. Her letter was a queen’s appeal, a mother’s cry, and a patriot’s warning.

**But the British refused,** they saw no heir, no queen, no right, only a territory to annex. In a thunderous show of force, the British attacked **Kittur** with **20,797 soldiers** and **437 guns** from the **Madras Native Horse Artillery** — a colonial avalanche against a principled resistance.

The first clash was thunderous — and **Kittur triumphed**. **St John Thackeray fell**, struck down in battle. **Amatur Balappa**, Chennamma’s fierce lieutenant, led the charge with unmatched bravery.

Two British officers — **Sir Walter Elliot** and **Mr. Stevenson** — were taken hostage.  
But Chennamma, in a gesture of dignity and diplomacy, **released them**. Her war was for justice, not vengeance.

Yet the British thirst for conquest was unquenched. They returned with renewed fury.  
In the second assault, even **Thomas Munro’s nephew** was slain — a grim echo of the cost of imperial ambition.

**Chennamma fought till the end.** Her sword did not falter. Her spirit did not bend.

  
But in **1829**, she was **captured** and **imprisoned**, where she eventually **passed away** — not defeated, but defiant. Many thought the resistance had ended. But one man refused to bow, her **faithful army chief**, **Sangoli Rayanna**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!A0LF!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3d5a7cad-235f-4645-90b5-5e0aef5090d4_283x512.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!A0LF!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3d5a7cad-235f-4645-90b5-5e0aef5090d4_283x512.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

On **August 15, 1798**, in the village of **Sangoli**, nestled in **Belagavi district**, Rayanna was born to **Bharamappa** and **Kanchamma** , a child destined to defy an empire. H**e belonged to the Kuruba community**, a proud pastoral warrior clan, known not just for tending flocks, but for defending kingdoms.

His bloodline was steeped in valor, his **grandfather, Rahgappa Veerappa Desai was**  
a legend in his own right, he earned the title **“Sāvira oṇṭe Saradāra”** — *Commander of a Thousand Horses*. His name galloped through history like the steeds he led.

His **father Bharamappa,** a fierce fighter who struck down the **tyrannical British officer Hook**, who had terrorized local villagers. His blade was not just sharp, it was just. Rayanna grew up not in comfort, but in **courage**. His lullabies were war songs. His playgrounds were training grounds. His heroes were his own kin.

When **Kittur fell** and **Rani Chennamma** was imprisoned, many saw it as the end.  
But **Rayanna** saw it as a beginning,h**e escaped**, carrying with him not just weapons ,but **Shivalingappa**, the adopted son of Chennamma, the heir she had fought to protect.

Rayanna’s mission was clear,**restore Shivalingappa to the throne, reclaim Kittur’s honor and keep the flame alive. With swift raids, surprise attacks, and guerrilla warfare, Rayanna turned the forests and hills around Kittur into a battlefield of resistance.**

His forces were small, but his spirit was vast. He struck like lightning ,unpredictable, unrelenting. T**he British retaliated** with economic cruelty,they imposed **heavy taxes** on the villages and lands Rayanna controlled, hoping to starve the rebellion.

But the people stood by him. Because Rayanna wasn’t just a warrior , he was **their protector**, **their voice**, **their hope**.**Villagers, farmers, herders, and artisans** ,all joined his cause.They weren’t soldiers by birth, but became warriors by necessity.

Rayanna trained them, armed them, and gave them purpose.His army wasn’t large ,but it was **loyal**, **nimble**, and **fierce**. **He waged guerrilla war**, striking from the shadows, vanishing into the forests. His knowledge of terrain was unmatched and his timing was ruthless.

The t**argets were chosen with precision**,British revenue officers,colonial .outposts and **corrupt Zamindars** who had betrayed their people for profit. Rayanna’s rebellion wasn’t just anti-British, it was **anti-oppression**. He punished those who exploited the poor, regardless of their allegiance.

**With his trusted aide Gajaveera**, Rayanna launched **relentless raids**.Together, they became a storm — unpredictable, unyielding, unforgettable.

Long before guerrilla warfare became a buzzword in revolutionary circles, **Sangoli Rayanna** was already mastering it. Like **Pazhassi Raja**, he used forests as fortresses.  
Like **Vasudev Balwant Phadke**, he turned **peasants into soldiers** and **rage into raids**.

Rayanna’s brilliance lay in **knowing the land, and trusting its people**.

**Local peasants** weren’t just foot soldiers. They were **scouts**, **strategists**, and **suppliers**.  
Their knowledge of terrain, secret paths, and British movements made Rayanna’s army **invisible yet invincible**.

He vandalized British offices,looted colonial treasuries and reclaimed wealth for the oppressed. Soon, his name became a **whisper of terror** in British corridors,*“Rayanna is near.”*And that was enough to send shivers.

The British could not capture him by force. So they turned to **treachery**. **Lakshmanraya**, Rayanna’s own uncle, betrayed him,a man who had fought for justice was undone by **blood turned bitter**.Rayanna was **captured**, along with **Shivalingappa**, the boy he had protected like a son, the heir he had hoped to crown.

On **January 26th, 1831**, at **Nandagad**, beneath a **banyan tree**, **Sangoli Rayanna was hanged**. But the tree did not forget.It grew into a **memorial**, its roots clutching the soil that once bore his footsteps.Today, that banyan stands not as a symbol of death —  
But as a **living monument to resistance**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Mdo5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8e727dd8-dd49-4eb0-81d1-d70a4316860f_1024x576.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Mdo5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8e727dd8-dd49-4eb0-81d1-d70a4316860f_1024x576.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> *I may die now but I will soon take birth again and come back to fight
> for my kingdom and the people until it is free from the clutches of
> British.*

In the villages of **North Karnataka**, his name is not just remembered ,it is **sung**, **invoked**, and **woven into the fabric of longing**. **Ballads echo** through the fields,songs of rebellion, loyalty, and sacrifice.Children grow up hearing tales of the warrior who defied an empire.

But his **most tender legacy** lives beneath the banyan tree at **Nandagad**.

**Women still come**, tying **cradles to its branches**,Whispering prayers for sons like Rayanna ,brave, loyal, and righteous. It is a ritual of remembrance.A gesture of generational hope.A mother's silent vow to raise courage.

The **Bengaluru City Railway Station**, one of India’s busiest, was officially renamed **Krantivira Sangolli Rayanna Station** in 2016. It’s more than a transport hub — it’s a **living tribute** to a man who once disrupted colonial routes with guerrilla raids. Now, his name graces the very arteries of modern India’s movement.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VZ45!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F20ee23c1-4dff-42df-bc58-e8ac790b0084_3899x2193.jpeg)

![Lalbagh flower show 2025: CM Siddaramaiah Opens I-Day Flower Show Honouring Chennamma, Rayanna](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VZ45!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F20ee23c1-4dff-42df-bc58-e8ac790b0084_3899x2193.jpeg "Lalbagh flower show 2025: CM Siddaramaiah Opens I-Day Flower Show Honouring  Chennamma, Rayanna")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This year’s **218th Independence Day Flower Show** at **Lalbagh Botanical Garden** is a breathtaking homage to **Rani Chennamma** and **Sangoli Rayanna**.

A **massive floral replica of Kittur Fort** crafted from roses and chrysanthemums. **Horse-mounted models** of Chennamma and Rayanna, standing proud in the glasshouse.

A **depiction of Rayanna’s execution** beneath the banyan tree — solemn, symbolic, and stirring. **Eight outdoor screens** displaying historical records, documentaries, and ballads about their lives.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
